title: Motor Neurone Disease
oneLiner: Progressive neurodegenerative disease of upper & lower motor neurons; causes weakness, respiratory failure, death within 2–5 years. Key ward issue: acute respiratory decompensation.

sections.Motor Neurone Disease (MND): **One-liner:** Progressive neurodegenerative disease of upper & lower motor neurons; causes weakness, respiratory failure, death within 2–5 years. Key ward issue: acute respiratory decompensation.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute on chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure** – key finding: morning headache + confusion + raised PaCO2 (>6.5 kPa) + low SpO₂ on air. This is not “just anxiety” in an MND patient.
- **Myasthenic crisis (mimic)** – key finding: *fluctuating* weakness (ptosis, diplopia, fatigable power) ± bulbar involvement. Does NOT improve with rest in MND. Test with ice pack (improves ptosis in myasthenia) – if positive, stop MND workup and start pyridostigmine.
- **Aspiration pneumonitis / choking** – key finding: sudden desaturation after food/secretions + witness of aspiration. Treat as foreign body / chemical pneumonitis first, then reassess.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  – Progressive, painless weakness (usually one limb → other limbs / bulbar)  
  – Fasciculations (especially tongue, thenar, quadriceps)  
  – Cramps, stiffness  
  – Dysarthria (slurred, nasal speech) → dysphagia (choking, weight loss)  
  – Dyspnoea / orthopnoea / morning headache (late sign of diaphragm weakness)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  – Mixed UMN + LMN in same region: e.g. brisk reflexes + wasted, fasciculating muscle  
  – Babinski sign + clonus (UMN) + muscle atrophy + fasciculations (LMN)  
  – No sensory loss; no extraocular muscle weakness (vs myasthenia)  
  – Bulbar: reduced palatal elevation, tongue fasciculations, jaw jerk hyperreflexia  
  – Respiratory: accessory muscle use, paradoxical breathing (diaphragm weakness), weak cough
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**  
  – **ALS (most common)** – typical UMN + LMN mixture in multiple regions  
  – Multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN) – pure LMN with *conduction block* on EMG; treatable (IVIG)  
  – Myasthenia gravis – fluctuating, fatigable weakness; improves with rest  
  – Cervical myelopathy + radiculopathy – sensory signs, neck pain, bowel/bladder dysfunction  
  – Primary lateral sclerosis (pure UMN) – very slow progression  
  – Kennedy’s disease (spinobulbar muscular atrophy) – X-linked; gynaecomastia, tremor, sensory loss  
  – Inclusion body myositis – asymmetrical finger flexor & quadriceps weakness; CK high, EMG myopathic  
- **Splitter:** presence of *sensory symptoms or signs* → NOT MND; think cervical myelopathy, Kennedy’s, MMN.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  – SpO₂, respiratory rate, auscultate for aspiration  
  – Spirometry (FVC sitting and supine) – **FVC < 50% predicted or >20% drop supine = high risk respiratory failure**  
  – ABG if SpO₂ <94% or symptoms of hypercapnia (headache, confusion)  
  – Swallow assessment (bedside water swallow test) – document risk  
- **Bloods:**  
  – FBC, U&E, LFT, CK (usually mildly elevated), TSH, B12, anti-GM1 antibodies (if MMN suspected)  
  – + NCS/EMG workup required (does not delay admission)  
- **Imaging:**  
  – MRI brain + whole spine (to exclude structural lesions like syrinx, tumour, myelopathy)  
- **Special tests:**  
  – Electromyography (EMG) / nerve conduction studies (NCS) – **diagnostic gold standard**  
    - Findings: denervation potentials (fibrillations, positive sharp waves), fasciculations, reduced motor unit recruitment  
    - Conduction block (if present → MMN, not ALS)  
  – Diagnostic criteria: **El Escorial revised criteria** – requires UMN + LMN signs in ≥1 body region, progression, and excluded mimics. “Definite ALS” = UMN+LMN in 3 regions.  
- **Never-miss test:** **EMG/NCS** – omission risks missing treatable MMN or myasthenia.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  – If SpO₂ <92% or hypercapnic (PaCO₂ >6.0): **Sit upright, give supplemental O₂ (target SpO₂ 94-98%), start non-invasive ventilation (NIV) via full face mask** with respiratory team / ICU.  
  – Do NOT give opioids or benzodiazepines for agitation – will worsen respiratory depression.  
  – If suspected aspiration: chest physio, suction, consider bronchodilators.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  – Confirm diagnosis with neurology team & EMG  
  – **Measure FVC daily** (if able); ABG if FVC drops or symptoms develop  
  – **Nutrition:** SALT review – modify diet consistency; if unsafe swallow → plan for gastrostomy (PEG/RIG)  
  – **Secretions:** glycopyrrolate (1-2 mg PO/SC TDS) or hyoscine patch  
  – **Spasticity:** baclofen (start 5 mg TDS, titrate)  
  – **Cramps:** quinine sulphate 200–300 mg nocte (off-label – check local policy)  
  – **Multidisciplinary team:** physio (chest clearance), OT, SALT, respiratory, palliative care  
  – **Advance care planning:** document ceilings of care (NIV vs intubation), DNACPR discussion  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – Disease-modifying: **Riluzole 50 mg BD** (slows progression – not for acute use)  
  – NIV (nocturnal BiPAP) for chronic respiratory failure  
  – Gastrostomy for nutrition  
  – Palliative care / hospice support  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  – Fluids: N/A – but ensure adequate oral intake; if NBM → IV fluids (e.g. 1L NS/24h)  
  – VTE prophylaxis: **enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD** (unless high bleed risk)  
  – Diet: “Soft/Risk of aspiration”; NBM if unsafe swallow  
  – Observations: NEWS2 + SpO₂ q4h; if on NIV → q1-2h initially
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  – *Riluzole*: 50 mg PO BD (tablet) – check LFTs at baseline & monthly for 3 months  
  – *Baclofen*: 5 mg PO TDS, increase by 5 mg every 3–5 days up to 20 mg TDS max (for spasticity)  
  – *Glycopyrrolate*: 1–2 mg PO TDS or 0.2–0.4 mg SC QDS (for sialorrhea)  
  – *Quinine sulfate*: 200–300 mg PO nocte (off-label; check local policy for cramps)  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  – Hyoscine hydrobromide transdermal patch (1 mg/72h) for sialorrhea  
  – Amitriptyline 10–25 mg PO nocte for sialorrhea (caution in elderly, confusion)  
  – Tizanidine (if baclofen ineffective) – start 2 mg TDS  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  – Baclofen withdrawal can cause life-threatening spasticity (always taper)  
  – Quinine: prolongs QT interval; avoid if on other QT-prolonging drugs  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  – Riluzole: caution in hepatic impairment; check LFTs  
  – Baclofen: reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min (accumulates → sedation)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  – *Baclofen*: accidental high dose (e.g., 10-20 mg TDS starting) → profound weakness, respiratory depression. Start low, go slow.  
  – *Opioids/sedatives* given without respiratory monitoring → fatal respiratory depression in MND with weak respiratory muscles.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  – Type 2 respiratory failure (hypercapnic encephalopathy)  
  – Aspiration pneumonia / acute airway obstruction  
  – Secretion retention → atelectasis  
- **Chronic:**  
  – Progressive weight loss (malnutrition); dysphagia → dehydration  
  – Contractures, pressure sores, pain (from muscle cramps & joint stiffness)  
  – Depression, anxiety, sleep disturbance  
  – Difficult decision-making about NIV/tracheostomy
sections.Prognosis:
- **ALS:** median survival 2–5 years from symptom onset; respiratory failure is main cause of death  
  – Bulbar-onset (20–30%) – shorter survival (~3 years)  
  – Limb-onset – slightly longer (~3–5 years)  
- **PLS/King’s variant** – slower progression (decades)  
- **30-day mortality:** low if admitted for respiratory support (NIV works in acute-on-chronic), but high if intubated (most do not wean)
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Check supine FVC** in every MND patient with new dyspnoea – a drop >20% predicts respiratory failure even with normal sitting FVC.  
- **Start NIV early** (nocturnal) when FVC <50% predicted or symptoms of nocturnal hypoventilation – can prolong survival & improve QoL.  
- **Refer to specialist MND clinic** within 48h of diagnosis – they coordinate riluzole, counselling, and advanced care planning.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving sedatives or opioids** without monitoring – one dose of morphine can cause fatal respiratory depression in a weakened patient.  
- **Assuming all LMN weakness is MND** – always request EMG to exclude MMN (treatable) or myasthenia (treatable).  
- **Delaying SALT and gastrostomy** – aspiration pneumonia is a common preventable cause of death; plan early PEG when FVC >50% (safer insertion).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  – Increasing breathlessness / orthopnoea / morning headache  
  – Choking episodes or voice changes  
  – Reduced consciousness or confusion (hypercapnia)  
  – Fever or green sputum (possible aspiration pneumonia)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  – Neurology MND clinic within 2 weeks  
  – Respiratory for NIV assessment (if not already on)  
  – Community palliative team / hospice support (refer early)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**  
  – NEWS2 ≥5 or any parameter triggers escalation  
  – SpO₂ <90% despite O₂ + NIV  
  – PaCO₂ >7.0 kPa or rising >1.0 kPa on ABG  
  – GCS <13 (CO₂ narcosis)  
  – Respiratory rate >25 or <8  
  – Weak cough with inability to clear secretions → urgent physio & suction; if fails → ICU for bronchoscopy?  
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  – FVC <30% predicted: likely need full ventilatory support discussion  
  – Acute respiratory failure in MND: often ceiling is NIV; only offer invasive ventilation if patient & family fully aware of likely inability to wean (discuss with senior before intubation)

rawMarkdown:
## Motor Neurone Disease (MND)
**One-liner:** Progressive neurodegenerative disease of upper & lower motor neurons; causes weakness, respiratory failure, death within 2–5 years. Key ward issue: acute respiratory decompensation.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute on chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure** – key finding: morning headache + confusion + raised PaCO2 (>6.5 kPa) + low SpO₂ on air. This is not “just anxiety” in an MND patient.
- **Myasthenic crisis (mimic)** – key finding: *fluctuating* weakness (ptosis, diplopia, fatigable power) ± bulbar involvement. Does NOT improve with rest in MND. Test with ice pack (improves ptosis in myasthenia) – if positive, stop MND workup and start pyridostigmine.
- **Aspiration pneumonitis / choking** – key finding: sudden desaturation after food/secretions + witness of aspiration. Treat as foreign body / chemical pneumonitis first, then reassess.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  – Progressive, painless weakness (usually one limb → other limbs / bulbar)  
  – Fasciculations (especially tongue, thenar, quadriceps)  
  – Cramps, stiffness  
  – Dysarthria (slurred, nasal speech) → dysphagia (choking, weight loss)  
  – Dyspnoea / orthopnoea / morning headache (late sign of diaphragm weakness)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  – Mixed UMN + LMN in same region: e.g. brisk reflexes + wasted, fasciculating muscle  
  – Babinski sign + clonus (UMN) + muscle atrophy + fasciculations (LMN)  
  – No sensory loss; no extraocular muscle weakness (vs myasthenia)  
  – Bulbar: reduced palatal elevation, tongue fasciculations, jaw jerk hyperreflexia  
  – Respiratory: accessory muscle use, paradoxical breathing (diaphragm weakness), weak cough

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**  
  – **ALS (most common)** – typical UMN + LMN mixture in multiple regions  
  – Multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN) – pure LMN with *conduction block* on EMG; treatable (IVIG)  
  – Myasthenia gravis – fluctuating, fatigable weakness; improves with rest  
  – Cervical myelopathy + radiculopathy – sensory signs, neck pain, bowel/bladder dysfunction  
  – Primary lateral sclerosis (pure UMN) – very slow progression  
  – Kennedy’s disease (spinobulbar muscular atrophy) – X-linked; gynaecomastia, tremor, sensory loss  
  – Inclusion body myositis – asymmetrical finger flexor & quadriceps weakness; CK high, EMG myopathic  
- **Splitter:** presence of *sensory symptoms or signs* → NOT MND; think cervical myelopathy, Kennedy’s, MMN.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  – SpO₂, respiratory rate, auscultate for aspiration  
  – Spirometry (FVC sitting and supine) – **FVC < 50% predicted or >20% drop supine = high risk respiratory failure**  
  – ABG if SpO₂ <94% or symptoms of hypercapnia (headache, confusion)  
  – Swallow assessment (bedside water swallow test) – document risk  
- **Bloods:**  
  – FBC, U&E, LFT, CK (usually mildly elevated), TSH, B12, anti-GM1 antibodies (if MMN suspected)  
  – + NCS/EMG workup required (does not delay admission)  
- **Imaging:**  
  – MRI brain + whole spine (to exclude structural lesions like syrinx, tumour, myelopathy)  
- **Special tests:**  
  – Electromyography (EMG) / nerve conduction studies (NCS) – **diagnostic gold standard**  
    - Findings: denervation potentials (fibrillations, positive sharp waves), fasciculations, reduced motor unit recruitment  
    - Conduction block (if present → MMN, not ALS)  
  – Diagnostic criteria: **El Escorial revised criteria** – requires UMN + LMN signs in ≥1 body region, progression, and excluded mimics. “Definite ALS” = UMN+LMN in 3 regions.  
- **Never-miss test:** **EMG/NCS** – omission risks missing treatable MMN or myasthenia.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  – If SpO₂ <92% or hypercapnic (PaCO₂ >6.0): **Sit upright, give supplemental O₂ (target SpO₂ 94-98%), start non-invasive ventilation (NIV) via full face mask** with respiratory team / ICU.  
  – Do NOT give opioids or benzodiazepines for agitation – will worsen respiratory depression.  
  – If suspected aspiration: chest physio, suction, consider bronchodilators.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  – Confirm diagnosis with neurology team & EMG  
  – **Measure FVC daily** (if able); ABG if FVC drops or symptoms develop  
  – **Nutrition:** SALT review – modify diet consistency; if unsafe swallow → plan for gastrostomy (PEG/RIG)  
  – **Secretions:** glycopyrrolate (1-2 mg PO/SC TDS) or hyoscine patch  
  – **Spasticity:** baclofen (start 5 mg TDS, titrate)  
  – **Cramps:** quinine sulphate 200–300 mg nocte (off-label – check local policy)  
  – **Multidisciplinary team:** physio (chest clearance), OT, SALT, respiratory, palliative care  
  – **Advance care planning:** document ceilings of care (NIV vs intubation), DNACPR discussion  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – Disease-modifying: **Riluzole 50 mg BD** (slows progression – not for acute use)  
  – NIV (nocturnal BiPAP) for chronic respiratory failure  
  – Gastrostomy for nutrition  
  – Palliative care / hospice support  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  – Fluids: N/A – but ensure adequate oral intake; if NBM → IV fluids (e.g. 1L NS/24h)  
  – VTE prophylaxis: **enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD** (unless high bleed risk)  
  – Diet: “Soft/Risk of aspiration”; NBM if unsafe swallow  
  – Observations: NEWS2 + SpO₂ q4h; if on NIV → q1-2h initially  

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  – *Riluzole*: 50 mg PO BD (tablet) – check LFTs at baseline & monthly for 3 months  
  – *Baclofen*: 5 mg PO TDS, increase by 5 mg every 3–5 days up to 20 mg TDS max (for spasticity)  
  – *Glycopyrrolate*: 1–2 mg PO TDS or 0.2–0.4 mg SC QDS (for sialorrhea)  
  – *Quinine sulfate*: 200–300 mg PO nocte (off-label; check local policy for cramps)  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  – Hyoscine hydrobromide transdermal patch (1 mg/72h) for sialorrhea  
  – Amitriptyline 10–25 mg PO nocte for sialorrhea (caution in elderly, confusion)  
  – Tizanidine (if baclofen ineffective) – start 2 mg TDS  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  – Baclofen withdrawal can cause life-threatening spasticity (always taper)  
  – Quinine: prolongs QT interval; avoid if on other QT-prolonging drugs  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  – Riluzole: caution in hepatic impairment; check LFTs  
  – Baclofen: reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min (accumulates → sedation)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  – *Baclofen*: accidental high dose (e.g., 10-20 mg TDS starting) → profound weakness, respiratory depression. Start low, go slow.  
  – *Opioids/sedatives* given without respiratory monitoring → fatal respiratory depression in MND with weak respiratory muscles.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  – Type 2 respiratory failure (hypercapnic encephalopathy)  
  – Aspiration pneumonia / acute airway obstruction  
  – Secretion retention → atelectasis  
- **Chronic:**  
  – Progressive weight loss (malnutrition); dysphagia → dehydration  
  – Contractures, pressure sores, pain (from muscle cramps & joint stiffness)  
  – Depression, anxiety, sleep disturbance  
  – Difficult decision-making about NIV/tracheostomy

### Prognosis
- **ALS:** median survival 2–5 years from symptom onset; respiratory failure is main cause of death  
  – Bulbar-onset (20–30%) – shorter survival (~3 years)  
  – Limb-onset – slightly longer (~3–5 years)  
- **PLS/King’s variant** – slower progression (decades)  
- **30-day mortality:** low if admitted for respiratory support (NIV works in acute-on-chronic), but high if intubated (most do not wean)

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Check supine FVC** in every MND patient with new dyspnoea – a drop >20% predicts respiratory failure even with normal sitting FVC.  
- **Start NIV early** (nocturnal) when FVC <50% predicted or symptoms of nocturnal hypoventilation – can prolong survival & improve QoL.  
- **Refer to specialist MND clinic** within 48h of diagnosis – they coordinate riluzole, counselling, and advanced care planning.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving sedatives or opioids** without monitoring – one dose of morphine can cause fatal respiratory depression in a weakened patient.  
- **Assuming all LMN weakness is MND** – always request EMG to exclude MMN (treatable) or myasthenia (treatable).  
- **Delaying SALT and gastrostomy** – aspiration pneumonia is a common preventable cause of death; plan early PEG when FVC >50% (safer insertion).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  – Increasing breathlessness / orthopnoea / morning headache  
  – Choking episodes or voice changes  
  – Reduced consciousness or confusion (hypercapnia)  
  – Fever or green sputum (possible aspiration pneumonia)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  – Neurology MND clinic within 2 weeks  
  – Respiratory for NIV assessment (if not already on)  
  – Community palliative team / hospice support (refer early)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**  
  – NEWS2 ≥5 or any parameter triggers escalation  
  – SpO₂ <90% despite O₂ + NIV  
  – PaCO₂ >7.0 kPa or rising >1.0 kPa on ABG  
  – GCS <13 (CO₂ narcosis)  
  – Respiratory rate >25 or <8  
  – Weak cough with inability to clear secretions → urgent physio & suction; if fails → ICU for bronchoscopy?  
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  – FVC <30% predicted: likely need full ventilatory support discussion  
  – Acute respiratory failure in MND: often ceiling is NIV; only offer invasive ventilation if patient & family fully aware of likely inability to wean (discuss with senior before intubation)
